sentences.text: At Freeport-McMoRan sites, completing this arc flash and NFPA 70E awareness training is what qualifies you to enter an MCC room or e-house in the first place — without that training, you are not permitted in these spaces at all, even with a site electrician present.
This module covers exactly how that access works once you're trained, so you know what to expect and what's expected of you.

How entry actually works here: Once you've completed this training, a site electrician opens the room or enclosure for you and gives you a site-specific briefing before you start work — covering things like higher voltage at a substation, unusual equipment in that specific room, or any other hazard particular to that location.
After that briefing, you can work on your own fire alarm and life-safety tasks in that space.
The general rule of thumb: fire protection professionals do not service or work on energized equipment above 60V — that work belongs to qualified site electricians.
If your task requires entering an area with exposed energized conductors above 60V, ask the electrician to de-energize the area first whenever possible.
This training gets the door opened and the hazard briefing done — it does not make you an electrician.
Complete this arc flash / NFPA 70E awareness training — this is the baseline requirement for entry at all.
Have a site electrician open the MCC room, e-house, or electrical room for you — you don't open these yourself.
If energized equipment above 60V is exposed in your work area, ask the electrician to de-energize it before you start.
Get the site-specific hazard briefing for that exact room before starting work (voltage level, layout quirks, anything unusual to that location).
Confirm you understand the briefing — ask questions before the electrician leaves, not after.
This training does not make you a qualified electrical worker — you do not open buckets, rack breakers, or service energized equipment above 60V.
Don't enter an MCC room or e-house without this training completed, even briefly, even for something that feels routine.
Don't assume "I'm just installing a detector" means you don't need to coordinate with the electrician about the energized equipment around you.
Don't skip the site-specific briefing because you've been in a similar room before — voltage and layout can differ room to room.
